notes.anchor: by those vetoes he has been plainly inflamed
notes.note: The intercessiones (tribunician vetoes) cast by Mark Antony and Q. Cassius Longinus against the Senate's decree of 7 January 49 BC; their flight to Caesar gave him his pretext for crossing the Rubicon, and Caelius says the affront still rankles.
deprecatio: the formal plea for mercy or intercession on a defeated man's behalf; Caelius warns that Caesar, for all his clemency to date, is in no mood to entertain such pleadings.
P. Cornelius Dolabella, who had married Cicero's daughter Tullia in 50 BC and was an active Caesarian; Caelius enlists his standing with Cicero as one more reason not to join Pompey.
The optimates were the conservative, senatorial 'best men' whose cause Pompey now led; Caelius warns Cicero against letting a point of honour about that party-label push him into a ruinous choice.

notes.anchor: the Spains --- which I report to you will be ours on Caesar's arrival
notes.note: The two Spanish provinces (Hispania Citerior and Ulterior), held for Pompey by his legates Afranius and Petreius; Caesar marched west in spring 49 and forced their surrender at Ilerda that summer, exactly as Caelius predicts.

notes.anchor: He is taking me with him to Spain
notes.note: Caelius, as Caesar's man, accompanied him on the Ilerda campaign of 49 BC; this is why he cannot detour to dissuade Cicero in person and resorts to this urgent letter.
